Gehenna: Gehenna refers to the Valley of Hinnom—a site outside Jerusalem once associated with child sacrifice and divine judgment (cf. Jeremiah 7:31). Jesus invokes it not to threaten literal flames, but to reveal the soul's most tragic fate: to be utterly severed from God's presence. Gehenna came to symbolize the torment of spiritual desolation—the inner wasteland where light is remembered but no longer found, where regret overwhelms, prayers go unanswered, and silence becomes unbearable. It mirrors the soul estranged from its Source, where every “almost” haunts the conscience and longing is sharpened by loss. Yet even such desolation carries a hidden mercy: to feel it is to still be capable of return. This is the destruction Jesus warns of—not just of body, but of relationship with God.
Hades: In biblical and Second Temple literature, Hades (Greek Hadēs) refers to the underworld or the realm of the dead, paralleling the Hebrew She'ol as the intermediate state where souls await final judgment (cf. Luke 16:23; Acts 2:27; Psalm 16:10). Distinct from Gehenna (the place of final punishment), Hades is portrayed as a temporary abode for both righteous and unrighteous dead, though often with spatial or experiential distinctions (cf. Luke 16:22–26). In Greco-Roman contexts, Hades was also personified as a deity ruling the underworld, but in biblical usage, the term primarily denotes the grave or unseen realm. Revelation 20:13–14 depicts Hades as ultimately giving up its dead and being cast into the lake of fire, highlighting its impermanence in eschatological terms. While not inherently a place of torment, New Testament usage increasingly associates Hades with judgment, reflecting evolving Jewish and Christian views on the afterlife during the Second Temple period.
Mammon: Aramaic: Mammon. Not merely money or possessions, but wealth personified as a spiritual power and rival master to God.
Raca: Aramaic insult meaning 'empty' or 'worthless.' Used to express contempt. In Jesus' context, a strong term of verbal abuse.
Denarius: A small silver coin used in Roman times, roughly equivalent to a day’s wage for a laborer.
Talent: A very large unit of money in the ancient world, representing about 6,000 denarii—many years' wages for a worker.
Gentile: Singular form of Gentiles. See Gentiles for full entry
Gentiles: In biblical contexts, Gentiles refers to all peoples not of Jewish descent, derived from the Hebrew term goyim (“nations”) and the Greek ethnē (used in the New Testament). The term often highlights the distinction between Israel and surrounding cultures in the Old Testament, reflecting God’s covenantal relationship with Abraham’s lineage (cf. Genesis 12:3) while also foretelling the inclusion of Gentiles in His redemptive plan (e.g., Isaiah 49:6). By the New Testament, “Gentiles” became central to the early Christian mission, as Jesus’ followers extended the Gospel beyond Jews, fulfilling promises that non-Israelites would share in salvation (cf. Acts 10:34–35; Romans 11:11–12). However, Gentiles were sometimes viewed negatively by Jews due to cultural and religious differences (e.g., Galatians 2:15), though positive examples like Ruth the Moabite (cf. Ruth 1:16–17) and the Roman centurion Cornelius (cf. Acts 10) demonstrate exceptions. Theologically, Gentiles symbolize the universal scope of God’s grace, bridging the Old Testament’s national identity with the New Testament’s vision of a multiethnic people of God (cf. Ephesians 2:11–13).
Centurion: A Roman military officer in command of about one hundred soldiers.
Leprosy: A term for various serious skin diseases in biblical times. Those afflicted were often isolated from society.
Righteousness: Faithful living in accordance with God’s will, which may result in persecution.
Scribe: Singular form of Scribes. See Scribes for full entry
Scribes: Scribes were professionals trained in reading, writing, and interpreting religious texts, playing a critical role in preserving and teaching the Law of Moses (cf. Ezra 7:6). In Second Temple Judaism, they became experts in biblical exegesis and oral traditions, often aligning with the Pharisees to promote strict observance of ritual purity (cf. Matthew 23:2–3). While Nehemiah and Ezra (cf. Ezra 10:1; Nehemiah 8:1) exemplify scribes positively, New Testament portrayals often critique their hypocrisy, legalism, and opposition to Jesus’ teachings (cf. Luke 5:21; Matthew 23:13–15). Scribes also served as legal advisors and members of the Sanhedrin, contributing to the religious and political structures of the time. Despite their negative portrayal in the Gospels, scribes were vital in maintaining scriptural accuracy and ensuring continuity in Jewish religious life (cf. 2 Chronicles 34:15), even as their rigid interpretations sometimes clashed with Jesus’ emphasis on mercy and inward righteousness.
